text.title: spec-requirements Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for spec-requirements. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Every FR must satisfy all five SMART criteria: Specific (one interpretation only), Measurable (testable pass/fail), Achievable (technically feasible), Relevant (traces to a user story), Time-bound (MoSCoW priority assigned).
rule.rationale: Ambiguous requirements are the largest source of rework. SMART forces precision at authoring time, not during implementation.

rule.statement: Use "System SHALL" imperative voice for every FR. Never use "should", "can", "may", or "will".
rule.rationale: "SHALL" is unambiguous in requirements engineering and signals a testable mandatory constraint.

rule.statement: Ban ambiguous words: "fast", "good", "easy", "support", "handle". Replace with quantified targets.
rule.rationale: These words cannot be tested — they produce requirements that pass review but fail verification.

rule.statement: Assign each FR exactly one MoSCoW priority: Must (cannot launch without), Should (important but not critical), Could (nice to have), Won't (explicitly excluded from this release).
rule.rationale: Without explicit prioritization, every requirement defaults to Must and MVP scope explodes.

rule.statement: Must requirements must not exceed 60% of total FR count. If they do, re-classify or split the feature.
rule.rationale: Enforcing this ratio preserves the MVP concept and prevents scope creep disguised as priority.

rule.statement: Every FR must reference at least one user story (US-X). Every Must FR must reference at least one acceptance criterion (AC-X).
rule.rationale: Traceability ensures every implemented requirement has a business reason and a verification condition.

rule.statement: Use sequential identifiers: FR-001, FR-002; US-001; AC-001. Never use section headings as identifiers.
rule.rationale: IDs are stable references across spec, design, and implementation documents. Headings change; IDs should not.

rule.statement: Write every AC in Given-When-Then format with specific values, not generic placeholders. "Given user is on registration page" is acceptable; "Given user enters valid data" is not.
rule.rationale: Specific values make ACs directly executable as BDD tests without interpretation.

rule.statement: Cover at minimum: happy path, error handling (validation failure), boundary condition, security scenario (unauthorized access or injection). Performance scenario required for any Must NFR.
rule.rationale: Agents and reviewers systematically skip boundary and security scenarios unless coverage is explicitly mandated.

rule.statement: Every NFR must specify a measurement method, not just a target. "Response time SHALL be < 500ms p95, measured server-side under 1000 concurrent users" is complete; "< 500ms" alone is not.
rule.rationale: A target without a measurement method is unverifiable — performance tests cannot be designed without knowing the conditions.

rule.statement: Before writing any requirement, create a Business Value Statement: [WHO] currently cannot [WHAT] because [WHY]. This results in [IMPACT]. By implementing [SOLUTION], we will [BENEFIT].
rule.rationale: Agents and reviewers need the "why" anchor before requirements; without it, Achievable and Relevant criteria cannot be validated.

rule.statement: Write use cases only for multi-step workflows with multiple actors or complex branching. Single-actor, linear flows do not need a use case — a user story with AC is sufficient.
rule.rationale: Use case overhead on simple features adds documentation burden without improving clarity.
